name: Camazotz
description: Camazotz, meaning "death bat" in Kʼicheʼ Maya, is a bat-like deity prominent in Mesoamerican mythology, particularly among the Maya and the Zapotec. He is often associated with darkness, night, death, and sacrifice. Representations of Camazotz are found in various forms of art, including pottery, sculptures, and codices. His image is often depicted as a humanoid figure with the head of a bat, wielding a sacrificial knife or other symbols of death. The deity's role evolved over time, with early depictions potentially linked to beneficial aspects like crop fertility, while later interpretations emphasized his more sinister association with the underworld and the dangers of the night. Camazotz embodies the fearsome aspects of the natural world, representing the unknown terrors lurking in the darkness and the inevitable presence of death within the cycle of life.
subtitle: Death Bat

keyMyths.title: The Popol Vuh and Camazotz
keyMyths.description: In the Popol Vuh, the Maya creation myth, the hero twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque encounter Camazotz and his bat-like servants in the House of Bats, one of the trials they face in Xibalba, the Maya underworld. Hunahpu is decapitated by Camazotz while he is sleeping, highlighting the deity's association with sudden death and the perils of the night. This episode underscores the twins' vulnerability and the dangers inherent in confronting the forces of the underworld.
keyMyths.source: Tedlock, Dennis. *Popol Vuh: The Definitive Edition.* Simon & Schuster, 1996.

keyMyths.title: Camazotz and Zapotec Art
keyMyths.description: Archaeological evidence from the Zapotec civilization reveals depictions of bat-like figures, potentially linked to Camazotz or related bat deities. These images, found on pottery and other artifacts, suggest the importance of bat imagery in Zapotec cosmology and ritual practices. The bat's association with the underworld and its nocturnal habits likely contributed to its symbolic significance in Zapotec religious beliefs. The precise relationship between these Zapotec bat figures and the later Maya Camazotz remains a topic of scholarly debate.
keyMyths.source: Caso, Alfonso, and Ignacio Bernal. *Urnas de Oaxaca*. Instituto Nacional de Antropología e Historia, 1952.

keyMyths.title: Camazotz as a Symbol of Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Camazotz is often depicted holding a sacrificial knife or other implements of ritual sacrifice, emphasizing his connection to violence and death. The deity's association with sacrifice likely reflects the importance of blood offerings in Mesoamerican religious practices, where human or animal sacrifices were believed to appease the gods and maintain cosmic balance. Camazotz embodies the fearsome power of the gods and the necessity of sacrifice to ensure the continuation of life.
keyMyths.source: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube. *An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya*. Thames & Hudson, 1993.
